coredata.dc:title: Preparation of photoluminescent carbon dots-embedded polyelectrolyte microcapsules
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2013 Chinese Society of Particuology and Institute of Process Engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Chinese Society of Particuology and Institute of Process Engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Xiaoling
Peng, Liming
Zong, Jie
Zhu, Yihua
coredata.dc:description: Two types of photoluminescent carbon dots (CDs)-embedded polyelectrolyte (PE) microcapsules were successfully prepared via the layer-by-layer (LbL) assembly approach on sacrificial templates. For the first type, the PE microcapsules with CDs embedded in the cavity were produced from assembly of five pairs of poly(sodium 4-styrensulfonate) (PSS) and poly(allylamine hydrochloride) (PAH) on CDs-pre-loaded meso-porous silica. For the second type, the PE microcapsules with CDs embedded in the wall were made of CDs and PAH, which were derived from SiO2 particles as templates. Microscope images confirmed the introduction of CDs into the two CDs-embedded microcapsules. These two microcapsules also retained the optical properties of free CDs. Photoluminescence spectra revealed that the two types of microcapsules had excitation-dependent photoluminescence behavior. When the excitation wavelength changed from 280 to 340nm, photoluminescence emission peak of the PE microcapsules with CDs embedded in the cavity shifts from 369 to 377nm, while for microcapsules with CDs embedded in the wall, emission peak shifts from 367 to 390nm. Due to low toxicity, good hydrophilicity and photoluminescence properties of CDs, these two kinds of photo-luminescent microcapsules have competitive potential for application in carriers for imaging, drug delivery and biosensors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon-dots
Layer-by-layer
Polyelectrolyte
Microcapsules
Photoluminescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of polyelectrolyte microcapsules with CDs embedded in the cavity
Preparation of polyelectrolyte microcapsules with CDs embedded in the wall
Preparation of polyelectrolyte microcapsules with CDs embedded in the cavity
Preparation of polyelectrolyte microcapsules with CDs embedded in the wall

item-info.ce:copyright: Chinese Society of Particuology and Institute of Process Engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: LbL assembly process of CDs-embedded polyelectrolyte microcapsules: (a) CDs in the cavity and (b) CDs in the wall. Capsules are not drawn to scale.
SEM images of CDs/MS composites (a), CDs/MS/(PAH/PSS)5PAH composites before (b) and after (c) dissolving the MS templates.
Microscopic image of CDs/(PAH/PSS)5 PAH microcapsules illuminated with UV-light.
Photoluminescent emission spectra of CDs/(PAH/PSS)5 PAH microcapsules dispersed in water at excitation wavelengths of 280, 300, 320 and 340nm, respectively.
SEM image of SiO2 (a), TEM images of SiO2@(PAH/CDs)4PAH/PSS/PAH with different magnifications (b and c) and SEM image of (PAH/CDs)4PAH/PSS/PAH microcapsules (d).
Microscopic images of (a) SiO2@(PAH/CDs)4PAH/PSS/PAH and (b) (PAH/CDs)4PAH/PSS/PAH microcapsules, illuminated with UV light.

Photoluminescent emission spectra of (PAH/CDs)4PAH/PSS/PAH microcapsules dispersed in water at excitation wavelengths of 280, 300, 320, 340 and 365nm, respectively. Inset: photograph of the solution of microcapsules taken under irradiation of 365nm UV-lamp.

head.ce:title: Preparation of photoluminescent carbon dots-embedded polyelectrolyte microcapsules

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for Ultrafine Materials of Ministry of Education, School of Materials Science and Engineering, East China University of Science and Technology, Shanghai 200237, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: ► Carbon dots-embedded polyelectrolyte microcapsules were prepared via layer-be-layer assembly. ► CDs-embedded microcapsules exhibited excitation-dependent photoluminescence behavior. ► CDs-embedded microcapsules can be used as bio-friendly carriers for drug delivery.

Two types of photoluminescent carbon dots (CDs)-embedded polyelectrolyte (PE) microcapsules were successfully prepared via the layer-by-layer (LbL) assembly approach on sacrificial templates. For the first type, the PE microcapsules with CDs embedded in the cavity were produced from assembly of five pairs of poly(sodium 4-styrensulfonate) (PSS) and poly(allylamine hydrochloride) (PAH) on CDs-pre-loaded meso-porous silica. For the second type, the PE microcapsules with CDs embedded in the wall were made of CDs and PAH, which were derived from SiO2 particles as templates. Microscope images confirmed the introduction of CDs into the two CDs-embedded microcapsules. These two microcapsules also retained the optical properties of free CDs. Photoluminescence spectra revealed that the two types of microcapsules had excitation-dependent photoluminescence behavior. When the excitation wavelength changed from 280 to 340nm, photoluminescence emission peak of the PE microcapsules with CDs embedded in the cavity shifts from 369 to 377nm, while for microcapsules with CDs embedded in the wall, emission peak shifts from 367 to 390nm. Due to low toxicity, good hydrophilicity and photoluminescence properties of CDs, these two kinds of photo-luminescent microcapsules have competitive potential for application in carriers for imaging, drug delivery and biosensors.

ce:section.ce:para: Over the past decades, polymeric multilayer microcapsules with tailored properties have attracted extensive interests for applications in drug delivery (Becker, Johnston, & Caruso, 2010; Esser-Kahn, Odom, Sottos, White, & Moore, 2011) and biosensors (McShane & Ritter, 2010). Especially, polyelectrolyte microcapsules with fluorescent agents are good microcapsule systems for drug delivery because polyelectrolytes provide flexibility and permeability while fluorescent particles endow optical properties (del Mercato et al., 2010), for easy detection by measuring their fluorescence signal with optical spectroscopy. Semiconductor quantum dots, a kind of fluorescent agent, have been used for imaging and ion sensing (Adamczak et al., 2012; Duan, Song, & Zhan, 2009) due to their excellent properties, such as, different emission wavelengths depending on nanoparticle size, nearly continuous excitation spectra with narrow emission bands and low photobleaching. CD-based quantum dots are very attractive semiconductor materials and show excellent superiority in biological imaging (Pellegrino et al., 2005). However, the cytotoxicity of the quantum dots still remains even if the quantum dots were dispersed in aqueous solution or embedded in microcapsules. Kirchner, Liedl, et al. (2005) reported the surface chemistry and aggregation state of toxic Cd2+ ions from quantum dots had an effect on cytotoxicity. Later, they also investigated cytotoxicity of nanoparticle-loaded polymer capsules and discovered that toxic Cd2+ were also released even if CdTe nanoparticles were embedded in capsules (Kirchner, Javer, et al., 2005). For their harm in health and environment, the extensive use of semiconductor quantum dots in biology remains limited (Bottrill & Green, 2011).

Hence, there is a demand to find non-toxic alternatives of semiconductor quantum dots. Photoluminescent carbon dots (CDs), a new type of “quantum dot”, have received more and more attention since CDs were discovered in 2004 (Xu et al., 2004). Various methods have been developed to prepare photoluminescent CDs (Zong, Zhu, Yang, Shen, & Li, 2011) or carbon nitride dots (Liu, Tian, et al., 2011; Liu, Wang, et al., 2011; Liu et al., 2012; Lu et al., 2012); they can be generally classified into two main groups, viz., top-down and bottom-up methods (Shen, Zhu, Yang, & Li, 2012). Due to their promising advantages such as facile production, tunable photoluminescence emission, photostability, chemical inertness, low cytotoxicity and excellent biocompatibility, as compared to semiconductor quantum dots (Baker & Baker, 2010; Yang et al., 2009), CDs have become attractive alternatives of semiconductor quantum dots in biochemical applications. Actually, to encapsulate CDs will be a meaningful work.Layer-by-layer (LbL) assembly is a classical and simple method for preparing these multilayer capsules. This technique is based on electrostatic interactions between oppositely charged materials with controllable number of layers and types of constitution of shells (Donath, Sukhorukov, Caruso, Davis, & Möhwald, 1998; Johnston, Cortez, Angelatos, & Caruso, 2006). By using this technique, two approaches have been developed to encapsulate fluorescent nanoparticles into microcapsules. One is to load fluorescent nanoparticles inside the cavity of microcapsules via LbL assembly on porous sacrificial templates which are pre-loaded with nanoparticles (Wang & Caruso, 2005). The other is to embed fluorescent nanoparticles into the walls of polyelectrolyte capsules through electrostatic adsorption or covalent binding, depending on charge features (Rivera Gil, del Mercato, del Pino, Munoz-Javier, & Parak, 2008). Our group has developed a simple method for preparing hydrophilic CDs by using citric acid as carbon source and mesoporous silica (MS) as support (Zong et al., 2011). This interesting method provides us not only a simple approach for obtaining CDs, but also an in situ synthesis method for preparing sacrificial templates functionalized with CDs (CDs/MS composites), which offers high loading efficiency of CDs in MS. On the other hand, CDs with surface functionalized by carboxylic/carbonyl moieties (Baker & Baker, 2010) are negatively charged and thus could stick to the positively charged polyelectrolyte to form the multilayers, thus offering a potential for preparation of photoluminescent CDs hybrid hollow microcapsules.

Herein, we have developed two types of photoluminescent microcapsules based on polyelectrolyte and CDs via LbL assembly: for the first type, CDs were embedded in the cavity of microcapsules, and for the second one, CDs were embedded in the wall of microcapsules. In the first approach, we demonstrated the formation of microcapsules with CDs embedded in the cavity via alternately poly(allylamine hydrochloride) (PAH) and poly (sodium 4-styrensulfonate) (PSS) deposition on the CDs/MS composites. In the second approach, we combined CDs and PAH to construct the shells of microcapsules, which were prepared via alternate deposition of PAH and CDs using SiO2 as sacrificial templates, followed by removal of the core. These two kinds of photoluminescent CDs-embedded microcapsules could possess good optical performance and lower cytotoxicity, and could thus be extensively applied in imaging, drug delivery, and biosensor.

Poly(allylamine hydrochloride) (PAH; molar mass: 70,000g/mol) and poly(sodium 4-styrensulfonate) (PSS; molar mass: 70,000g/mol) used as polyelectrolyte materials were purchased from Sigma–Aldrich. All other chemicals were purchased from Shanghai Lingfeng Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. All these reagents were used without any further purification. The concentrations of PAH and PSS solutions were 1.0mg/mL with 0.5mol/L NaCl and 2.0mg/mL with 0.5mol/L NaCl, respectively. Pure water (18MΩcm) was obtained from a water purification system offered by Shanghai Winner Environmental Technology Co., Ltd.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of polyelectrolyte microcapsules with CDs embedded in the cavity
ce:section.ce:para: CDs-embedded mesoporous silica (denoted as CDs/MS) composites were synthesized using MS as nanoreactors and citric acid as carbon source according to our previous work with slight modification (Zong et al., 2011).

Polyelectrolyte microcapsules were prepared by LbL assembly of PAH and PSS as reported in literature (Kreft, Javier, Sukhorukov, & Parak,2007; Zhang, Zhu, Yang, & Li, 2010). Before adsorption, CDs/MS composites needed to be washed one time with pure water in order to prevent particles aggregation. Then the positively charged PAH and the negatively charged PSS were alternately deposited onto the negatively charged CDs/MS composites. All adsorption procedures were carried out at ambient temperature and adsorption time was 10min. After each adsorption step, the superfluous polyelectrolyte in solution was removed by centrifugation and washing with pure water. After CDs/MS composites were wrapped by five bilayers of PAH/PSS and another layer of PAH were adsorbed in order to protect the composites, CDs/MS/(PAH/PSS)5PAH composites were produced. Following several exposure to a hydrofluoric acid (HF)/ammonium fluoride (NH4F) buffer (pH=5) and washing with pure water for three cycles, the decomposition products of MS were discarded. Thus, CDs were released into the interior of the microcapsules and polyelectrolyte microcapsules with CDs embedded in the cavity, denoted as CDs/(PAH/PSS)5 PAH microcapsules, were obtained. These microcapsules were stored in pure water at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of polyelectrolyte microcapsules with CDs embedded in the wall
ce:section.ce:para: PAH and CDs were alternately deposited on SiO2 via layer-by-layer assembly. The negatively charged SiO2 (∼330nm) used here were prepared by a modified Stöber method (Stöber & Fink, 1968). The homogeneous yellow CDs solution (0.002g/mL, pH=8) was derived from CDs/MS composites as follows without any further treatment.

First, SiO2 microparticles (0.3g) were dispersed in 6mL of PAH solution with ultrasonic vibrations for 6min and shaking for 10min before centrifugation and washing with pure water. And then they were dispersed in 6mL of CDs solution for 6min and shaking for another 10min, followed by centrifugation and washing with pure water. After repeating alternate adsorption for 4 times, multilayer shells made of PAH and CDs were obtained. To protect the CDs-embedded composites, another three layers of poly-electrolyte were adsorbed. The obtained products were SiO2@(PAH/CDs)4PAH/PSS/PAH composites.

Then, the SiO2@(PAH/CDs)4PAH/PSS/PAH composites were dispersed and exposed to HF/NH4F buffer solution (pH=5) and shaken for 3min. This etching procedure was repeated twice to remove the SiO2 core. After that, washing with pure water was repeated several times until the solution system became neutral. Finally, the resulting (PAH/CDs)4PAH/PSS/PAH microcapsules were produced.
The detail preparation of the CDs-embedded microcapsules is schematically shown in Fig. 1 .

The microstructures of the samples were examined using scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JEOL JSM-6360LV) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL JEM 2011). Microscopy images of the multilayer microcapsules were recorded on a fluorescence inverse microscope (Nikon TE-2000 U). Photoluminescence spectra were measured on a UV-VIS-NIR fluorescence spectrophotometer (Jobin Yvon Fluorolog-3-P).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of polyelectrolyte microcapsules with CDs embedded in the cavity
ce:section.ce:para: Based on previous report (Zong et al., 2011), confined pores of MS spheres can be used as supports for synthesis of CDs. Citric acid was immobilized in the pores of MS and MS serves to localize the growth of CDs by blocking nanoparticles agglomeration during high-temperature calcination. This approach offered high loading-efficiency of CDs in MS and CDs could be conveniently obtained. Hence, in this work, we used MS as support for the formation of CDs/MS composites and CDs. Fig. 2(a) shows the SEM image of CDs/MS. CDs/MS composites display uniform dispersion and are spherical in shape with an average diameter of 1.3μm.

Five bilayers of (PAH/PSS) were coated onto the surface of CDs/MS to form CDs/MS/polyelectrolyte composites. Fig. 2 shows the morphology of CDs/MS/(PAH/PSS)5PAH composites and corresponding microcapsules. The CDs/MS/(PAH/PSS)5PAH composites with average size of 1.8μm exhibit similar appearance of CD/MS composites (Fig. 2(a) and (b)). After treatment with HF/NH4F, CDs were trapped in the cavity of the microcapsules and hollow CDs/(PAH/PSS)5 PAH microcapsules were obtained, as shown in Fig. 2(c).

Microscopic image (Fig. 3 ) further confirmed the formation of the photoluminescent CDs/(PAH/PSS)5 PAH microcapsules. As shown in Fig. 3, the microcapsules are in good dispersion. Furthermore, blue photoluminescence of CDs/(PAH/PSS)5 PAH microcapsules is still observed under the excitation of UV-light though the concentration of the loaded-CDs was low due to loss of CDs during the LbL process.

To further investigate the optical property of the microcapsules embedded with CDs in the cavity, photoluminescence emission spectra of as-prepared microcapsules were measured (Fig. 4 ). These CDs-embedded microcapsules keep optical performance in agreement with CDs well and also exhibit excitation-dependent photoluminescence behavior. In detail, photoluminescence emission peak shifts to longer wavelength from 370 to 377nm as the excitation wavelength is changed from 280 to 340nm. Furthermore, we can also observe that there is a little blue shift in the peak emission of CDs/(PAH/PSS)5 PAH microcapsules (377nm) compared with that in CDs aqueous solution (420nm) upon irradiation by 340nm (not shown). This may be attributed to the presence of polyelectrolyte multilayers, which could disturb the transmission of excitation light and emission light of CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of polyelectrolyte microcapsules with CDs embedded in the wall
ce:section.ce:para: SiO2 colloidal particles were used as templates due to such advantages as facile production, low aggregation and high monodispersity (Peyratout & Dahne, 2004). CDs possess carboxyl moieties on their surface, resulting in hydrophilicity and negative charging of CDs without further modification. The as-prepared CDs (approximately 3–4nm) released from the CDs/MS composites were used. Moreover the ζ-potential value of these CDs was measured to be −38.3mV, which was suitable for the fabrication of multilayered films using the LbL technique. So, it is easy to embed CDs into the multilayered shell via using LbL technique based on electrostatic attraction between the negatively charged CDs and the positively charged PAH.

Fig. 5 shows the morphologies of bare SiO2, the corresponding SiO2@(PAH/CDs)4PAH/PSS/PAH composites and hollow microcapsules after the removal of the core. Fig. 5(a) reveals that the bare SiO2 are uniform and spherical with approximate size of 330nm. Fig. 5(b) and (c) show that uniform layers are obtained, indicating that PAH/CDs multilayers are well deposited on the surface of SiO2. The average thickness of PAH/CDs multilayers is estimated to be 10nm. Fig. 5(d) shows SEM image of the collapsed microcapsules with a smooth surface morphology. The collapse may be due to weak mechanical strength from the ultrathin shell consisting of CDs and PAH, which is similar to capsules fabricated from solely polyelectrolyte species (Petrov, Antipov, & Sukhorukov, 2003).

Microscope images (Fig. 6 ) show these SiO2@(PAH/CDs)4PAH/PSS/PAH composites and their microcapsules, further indicating the successful coating of CDs on the surface of SiO2. From Fig. 6(a), SiO2@(PAH/CDs)4PAH/PSS/PAH composites exhibit strong blue photoluminescence when illuminated with UV-light, while (PAH/CDs)4PAH/PSS/PAH microcapsules show weaker blue luminescence (Fig. 6(b)), which may be attributed to the loss of CDs during the removal of SiO2.

Optical properties of (PAH/CDs)4PAH/PSS/PAH microcapsules were also investigated. Fig. 7 shows photoluminescence emission spectra and photograph of these (PAH/CDs)4PAH/PSS/PAH microcapsules. Blue photoluminescence could be easily seen with naked eyes upon irradiation by a 365nm UV-lamp. And it is interesting to see that peak emission of CDs in the multilayer shells is 390nm while that in the cavity of microcapsules is 377nm upon the same irradiation by 340nm. This difference may be attributed to the interaction between CDs and PAH layer or the change of surface state of CDs, which is similar to CdS quantum dot in the PAH layers (Rogach et al., 2000).

Moreover, (PAH/CDs)4PAH/PSS/PAH microcapsules also exhibit excitation-dependent photoluminescence behavior similar to that of CDs in aqueous solution. In detail, when the excitation wavelength is changed from 280 to 340nm, photoluminescence emission peak shifts from 367 to 390nm. Therefore, these photoluminescent (PAH/CDs)4PAH/PSS/PAH microcapsules, of which different signals can be detected by easily adjusting irradiation wavelength, are suitable carriers for drug delivery and biosensors. Finally, both microcapsules possessed good stability at room temperature. And their photoluminescence intensity exhibited no obvious decrease after a month.

In summary, two kinds of CDs-embedded polyelectrolyte microcapsules were successfully prepared via LbL technique. One, CDs/(PAH/PSS)5PAH microcapsules were fabricated by alternate adsorption of PSS and PAH onto the CDs/MS composites; the other, (PAH/CDs)4PAH/PSS/PAH microcapsules were produced by alternate deposition of PAH and CDs on the sacrificial SiO2 template. The two resulting CDs-embedded microcapsules emitted blue fluorescence upon irradiation by UV-light and exhibited excitation-dependent photoluminescence behavior, similar to that of CDs in aqueous solution. Moreover, for the (PAH/CDs)4PAH/PSS/PAH microcapsules, CDs loading on the shells can be tuned by varying the CDs concentration or layers, thus allowing control of photoluminescence intensity of the microcapsules. In addition, photo-induced CDs showed electron transfer and redox properties (Wang et al., 2009), and other biocompatible materials could replace PAH and PSS (Shchepelina, Drachuk, Gupta, Lin, & Tsukruk, 2011). Therefore the CDs-embedded microcapsules may extend their applications in a broad field, such as imaging, drug delivery, biosensors, and microreactors for photocatalysis.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank the National Natural Science Foundation of China (20925621, 20976054, and 21176083), the Special Projects for Nanotechnology of Shanghai (11nm0500800), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities, the Program for Changjiang Scholars and Innovative Research Team in University (IRT0825), and the Shanghai Leading Academic Discipline Project (Project Number: B502), for financial supports.
